does: This paper introduces the Adversarial Prompt Disentanglement (APD) framework, a proactive defense mechanism designed to identify and neutralize malicious components in input prompts before they reach LLMs. The framework combines three technical innovations: a mutual information-based semantic decomposition method that statistically isolates adversarial from benign prompt components, a graph-based intent classification approach using spectral analysis to detect malicious patterns in prompt semantics, and a lightweight transformer-based classifier trained on real-world datasets of toxic and jailbreaking prompts. The system aims to defend against attacks like jailbreaking and prompt injection by detecting adversarial intent with high efficiency and accuracy before harmful outputs can be generated.
connects: This work builds on growing research into adversarial robustness of LLMs and safety mechanisms against prompt-based attacks. It extends prior work on prompt injection defenses and jailbreaking mitigation by moving beyond post-hoc filtering to semantic-level decomposition. The approach integrates concepts from adversarial machine learning, information theory, and graph-based anomaly detection, combining these perspectives to address limitations in existing defense strategies that struggle with semantic ambiguities. The mutual information-based decomposition draws on established principles for disentangling independent factors, while the spectral graph analysis approach leverages techniques from network analysis to identify structural patterns indicative of malicious intent.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more robust LLM deployment in security-critical applications by providing a proactive defense layer independent of the underlying model. Future work could extend semantic decomposition to handle increasingly sophisticated adversarial prompt engineering, incorporate feedback mechanisms to improve detection as new attack patterns emerge, and develop theoretical guarantees on robustness margins. The approach may inspire hybrid defenses combining semantic analysis with model-level safety mechanisms, enable safer multimodal systems by extending graph-based detection to other modalities, and support development of certified defenses with provable guarantees against certain prompt attack classes. The framework also points toward foundational research on what makes prompts semantically adversarial and how to measure adversarial semantic distance in LLM input spaces.
